rationale: Title X is clean, well-structured, and immediately communicates the subject (intraprocedural pneumopericardium during epicardial ablation for ventricular arrhythmias) and the three key reporting dimensions (incidence, clinical effects, long-term prognosis). It fits JICE's preference for concise, structured titles without sacrificing content. Title Y attempts to be more specific by adding procedural context (dry pericardiocentesis), a comparator group (non-pneumopericardium), and sample size (n=86), which does raise raw informativeness. However, at that length the title becomes unwieldy and difficult to parse at a glance; the run-on noun phrases and the comparator clause buried mid-title hurt clarity and editorial appeal. JICE typically rewards precision and brevity over exhaustive detail-cramming.
loser_weakness: Title Y is overloaded: it stacks too many qualifiers (dry pericardiocentesis, hemodynamic effects, long-term outcomes, comparator group, single-center cohort, n=86) into one sentence, degrading readability and editorial appeal without a proportional gain in scientific precision. The sample size and design details belong in the abstract, not the title.
